summary: Azure Load Balancer is a Layer 4 (OSI) service that distributes incoming TCP/UDP network traffic across backend VMs or VM scale sets. It supports both public (internet-facing) and internal (private network) configurations. It provides high availability, scalability to millions of flows, and low-latency pass-through load balancing.

key_concepts.explanation: Handles inbound internet traffic to VMs and translates private IPs to public IPs for outbound connections from VMs.
Distributes traffic within a virtual network, used for private connectivity scenarios like hybrid on-premises access.
The current production-grade SKU built on Zero Trust model; closed to inbound connections by default, requires NSGs to allow traffic. Has SLA and supports availability zones.
Retired September 30, 2025. Was open to internet by default, had no SLA, and offered no charge. Must migrate to Standard.
Specialized SKU for chaining with third-party network virtual appliances (NVAs); enables transparent bump-in-the-wire scenarios.
Mechanisms that check backend instance health; load balancer only sends traffic to instances that pass health checks.
Configuration that defines how inbound traffic from the frontend is distributed to backend pool instances.
A rule type that load balances all TCP and UDP ports simultaneously, used for NVA scenarios with internal load balancer.
The group of VMs or VM scale sets that receive distributed traffic from the load balancer frontend.
Standard Load Balancer can provide SNAT (source network address translation) for VMs to reach the internet outbound.

exam_focus_points: Azure Load Balancer operates at OSI Layer 4 (transport layer), not Layer 7 — use Application Gateway or Front Door for Layer 7/HTTP-aware load balancing.
Basic Load Balancer was retired September 30, 2025; exam questions may test knowledge of differences and migration requirements.
Standard Load Balancer is closed to inbound connections by default; NSGs must explicitly allow traffic.
Basic Load Balancer was open to the internet by default — a key security difference from Standard.
Public load balancer handles both inbound internet traffic AND outbound SNAT for VMs.
Internal load balancer is used for traffic within a VNet or hybrid (on-premises) scenarios.
Standard Load Balancer supports availability zones for zone-redundant or zonal deployments; Basic does not.
High-availability ports rule balances all ports simultaneously — only available on internal Standard Load Balancer.
Azure Load Balancer does not store customer data; all processing is real-time.
Gateway Load Balancer is used for transparent insertion of NVAs, not general load balancing.
Standard Load Balancer has an SLA; Basic Load Balancer had no SLA.
Health probes must be configured correctly — unhealthy instances are removed from rotation automatically.
common_mistakes: Confusing Azure Load Balancer (Layer 4) with Application Gateway (Layer 7) — Load Balancer cannot route based on URL paths or HTTP headers.
Assuming Standard Load Balancer is open to the internet by default — it is closed by default and requires NSG rules to permit traffic.
Forgetting that Basic Load Balancer has been retired (Sept 30, 2025) and exam scenarios may require recommending migration to Standard.
Thinking Load Balancer can distribute traffic across Azure regions — cross-region load balancing requires Azure Front Door or Traffic Manager.
Confusing Internal Load Balancer with a VPN Gateway — Internal LB distributes traffic within a VNet, it does not create VPN tunnels.
Overlooking that outbound connectivity (SNAT) must be explicitly configured with Standard Load Balancer, unlike Basic which provided implicit outbound.
Mixing up Gateway Load Balancer (for NVA chaining) with Standard Load Balancer used for general workloads.
